headline: Week 2 Cheat Sheet
dek: Pre-draw edition for 13-19 April 2026. Week 2 shifts to a two-event clay slate with Barcelona and Munich, which means fantasy decisions get sharper: fewer true ceiling plays, more ranking-based pricing mistakes, and a bigger edge if you react quickly after Saturday’s draw.

columns.heading: Barcelona
columns.body: The official ATP preview lists Alcaraz, Musetti, Alex de Minaur, Felix Auger-Aliassime, Casper Ruud, Karen Khachanov and Andrey Rublev in the field. This is the more technical clay event of the two. For fantasy, it is the better slate for stable baseliners, straight-set favorites and safe Bonus Ball builds.

columns.heading: Munich
columns.body: The official ATP preview lists Zverev, Ben Shelton, Joao Fonseca, Alexander Bublik, Stefanos Tsitsipas and Flavio Cobolli. Munich sets up as the better fantasy event for aggressive servers, volatile scorers and players who can stack ace bonuses quickly.

cards.title: What Matters This Week
paragraphs: ATP Fantasy is built on a 100-credit budget with six starters, two alternates, and one Bonus Ball player who scores double. The scoring format rewards deep runs, but Week 2 also puts extra weight on ace upside, straight-set wins, and avoiding double-fault chaos.

Barcelona and Munich are both ATP 500s, so the round-winning value is strong at both events. Because prices are ranking-driven, the edge is usually not in finding the most famous player. It is in finding the player whose current clay level is stronger than his price.
cards.subtitle: Best Week 2 Angles
bullets: Back elite clay movers and front-runners in Barcelona.
Target Munich players with serve-plus-forehand scoring upside.
Watch Monte-Carlo workload carefully before locking Barcelona names.
Use the draw to separate safe quarter paths from trap quarters.

items.body: Sinner carried a run of 37 consecutive sets won at ATP Masters 1000 level into Monte-Carlo before Tomas Machac finally took one off him. Even with the streak snapped, he still played through to the quarter-finals, which is a reminder of how high his fantasy floor is.

Alcaraz pushed his clay-court winning streak to 15 matches by fighting past Tomas Martin Etcheverry after dropping a messy second set. That is exactly the kind of deep-run signal that keeps him at the top of the Bonus Ball board.
Bublik beat Jiri Lehecka in 75 minutes and ATP noted that he is now 18-5 on clay since the start of 2025. If fantasy managers still treat him like a surface flier, there is leverage in that gap.

Zverev escaped Cristian Garin after trailing 2-5 in the deciding set in his first clay event of the season. The comeback preserved his ceiling, but it also underlined that he is not a no-risk click if the Munich draw gets tricky.

Alcaraz is the cleanest Week 2 ceiling play if his draw is normal. He headlines Barcelona, entered the season as ATP Fantasy’s highest-priced player at 40 credits, and brought serious clay form into Monte-Carlo. If he lands away from the other Barcelona heavyweights, he is the default Bonus Ball.

Zverev is the Munich anchor. He is ATP Fantasy’s No. 3 price at launch and arrives with deep-run equity after reaching the Australian Open, Indian Wells and Miami semis earlier this season. The caveat is that his first clay week in Monte-Carlo looked uneven, so the draw matters more for him than for Alcaraz.

De Minaur is not the highest-upside ace play, but he is one of the best candidates to string together straight-set wins in a 500 if the bracket breaks well. He fits lineups that want a safer path without paying full superstar tax.
The Monte-Carlo loss to Valentin Vacherot will scare some managers off, which is useful if the market overreacts. Barcelona is a better setup for Musetti than a one-match sample says.

Munich is a better fantasy fit for Shelton than Barcelona because the ace ceiling matters more there. If his price sits below the elite names, he is one of the best scoring-profile plays on the board.

Rublev is a classic buy-low clay name if the draw is not brutal. The ATP field article confirms he is in Barcelona, and this is the kind of week where his ranking-driven price can beat his public sentiment.

Fonseca is the Week 2 upside value if ATP Fantasy still prices him below the established top tier. ATP reported him at World No. 40 during Monte-Carlo, and he already owns clay credibility after winning Buenos Aires in 2025. He is a high-variance play, but the fantasy scoring system rewards exactly his kind of ace-plus-upset profile.
Munich is an interesting reset spot for Tsitsipas. He has clear clay equity, and because his ranking is no longer at peak level, his price should be more manageable than his surface history suggests.

Barcelona rewards first-strike tennis if the path is right. Auger-Aliassime has enough serve bonus upside to matter, and he is often more useful in fantasy than in outright-title conversations because one hot week can pile up fast points.

Bublik is not a safe play, but Munich is exactly where volatility can become leverage. ATP noted he entered Monte-Carlo on an 18-5 clay run dating back to the start of 2025. If he avoids Zverev’s section, he is a real differentiator.
Ruud is always tempting on clay, but he retired against Auger-Aliassime in Monte-Carlo. Until there is clarity on his status, he is a dangerous Week 2 click.

items.tag: Anyone with a brutal Monte-Carlo finish
items.body: Barcelona begins immediately after Monte-Carlo. If a player reaches the final weekend in Monaco and then draws a dangerous opener, the fatigue penalty is real.

items.tag: Serve-heavy names in Barcelona bad quarters
items.body: Barcelona is less forgiving than Munich for players who rely on one dominant pattern. If they draw elite returners early, the ace bonus alone may not save them.

footer_note: Pre-draw edition based on information available on 10 April 2026. This page will be updated after the official Week 2 draws are released on Saturday, 11 April 2026.
archive_summary: Pre-draw ATP Fantasy read for Barcelona and Munich with bonus-ball, value, and trap guidance before the official draws.
